metadata.name: Edmund Bergler Psychological Database
metadata.description: Edmund Bergler's psychoanalytic positions. Total: 257 positions.
metadata.note: Bergler's views on homosexuality (Section VIII) are included for historical/archival purposes only. These positions have been repudiated by contemporary psychology, which recognizes homosexuality as a normal variant of human sexuality.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: All neuroses ultimately derive from disturbances in the earliest oral phase of development—the infant's relationship with the mother's breast.
positions.text_evidence: All neuroses ultimately derive from disturbances in the earliest oral phase of development—the infant's relationship with the mother's breast.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The infant experiences the breast as omnipotent and itself as the controller of that omnipotence; inevitable frustration shatters this illusion.
positions.text_evidence: The infant experiences the breast as omnipotent and itself as the controller of that omnipotence; inevitable frustration shatters this illusion.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: This frustration produces 'oral pessimism'—a fundamental conviction that the world is denying, refusing, and withholding.
positions.text_evidence: This frustration produces 'oral pessimism'—a fundamental conviction that the world is denying, refusing, and withholding.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The infant responds to oral frustration with aggression toward the mother, but this aggression is immediately turned inward due to helplessness and dependency.
positions.text_evidence: The infant responds to oral frustration with aggression toward the mother, but this aggression is immediately turned inward due to helplessness and dependency.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: This internalized aggression becomes the nucleus of the punishing superego.
positions.text_evidence: This internalized aggression becomes the nucleus of the punishing superego.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The 'basic neurosis' is the unconscious need to recreate and re-experience early oral disappointment—to prove again and again that the world refuses.
positions.text_evidence: The 'basic neurosis' is the unconscious need to recreate and re-experience early oral disappointment—to prove again and again that the world refuses.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: All later developmental stages (anal, phallic, genital) are overlays on this oral foundation; apparent Oedipal conflicts often mask deeper oral injuries.
positions.text_evidence: All later developmental stages (anal, phallic, genital) are overlays on this oral foundation; apparent Oedipal conflicts often mask deeper oral injuries.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Psychic masochism is the unconscious seeking of psychic pain, defeat, humiliation, and failure—not for pleasure in pain itself, but as a solution to deeper conflicts.
positions.text_evidence: Psychic masochism is the unconscious seeking of psychic pain, defeat, humiliation, and failure—not for pleasure in pain itself, but as a solution to deeper conflicts.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Psychic masochism is distinct from conscious suffering; the masochist consciously wants success and happiness but unconsciously arranges failure and misery.
positions.text_evidence: Psychic masochism is distinct from conscious suffering; the masochist consciously wants success and happiness but unconsciously arranges failure and misery.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The masochist does not enjoy suffering—he suffers from suffering but cannot stop arranging it.
positions.text_evidence: The masochist does not enjoy suffering—he suffers from suffering but cannot stop arranging it.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Masochism represents a 'lesser evil' solution: by choosing one's own suffering, one maintains an illusion of control and avoids the greater terror of unpredictable external harm.
positions.text_evidence: Masochism represents a 'lesser evil' solution: by choosing one's own suffering, one maintains an illusion of control and avoids the greater terror of unpredictable external harm.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Masochistic suffering also appeases the superego's demand for punishment, temporarily reducing unconscious guilt.
positions.text_evidence: Masochistic suffering also appeases the superego's demand for punishment, temporarily reducing unconscious guilt.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Everyone has masochistic tendencies; the question is only of degree and the specific form they take.
positions.text_evidence: Everyone has masochistic tendencies; the question is only of degree and the specific form they take.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Masochism expresses itself in all domains: love, work, creativity, money, health, and social relations.
positions.text_evidence: Masochism expresses itself in all domains: love, work, creativity, money, health, and social relations.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Neurotic symptoms and character traits operate through a characteristic triad of unconscious mechanisms.
positions.text_evidence: Neurotic symptoms and character traits operate through a characteristic triad of unconscious mechanisms.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: First mechanism: The unconscious ego creates or provokes a situation of refusal, rejection, or mistreatment—recreating the original oral disappointment.
positions.text_evidence: First mechanism: The unconscious ego creates or provokes a situation of refusal, rejection, or mistreatment—recreating the original oral disappointment.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Second mechanism: The ego responds to this (self-created) disappointment with aggression and indignation, experiencing itself as the innocent victim of injustice.
positions.text_evidence: Second mechanism: The ego responds to this (self-created) disappointment with aggression and indignation, experiencing itself as the innocent victim of injustice.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Third mechanism: This aggression, being directed at an internal maternal image, triggers guilt and the need for punishment, which the symptom or character pattern itself provides.
positions.text_evidence: Third mechanism: This aggression, being directed at an internal maternal image, triggers guilt and the need for punishment, which the symptom or character pattern itself provides.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The neurotic is thus simultaneously the criminal, the victim, and the executioner—all roles are played unconsciously.
positions.text_evidence: The neurotic is thus simultaneously the criminal, the victim, and the executioner—all roles are played unconsciously.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The conscious experience is only of victimization; the unconscious authorship of one's own suffering remains hidden.
positions.text_evidence: The conscious experience is only of victimization; the unconscious authorship of one's own suffering remains hidden.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The superego forms primarily from internalized maternal prohibitions and the infant's own aggression turned inward.
positions.text_evidence: The superego forms primarily from internalized maternal prohibitions and the infant's own aggression turned inward.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The superego is not a copy of external authorities but a distorted, magnified, sadistic caricature of them.
positions.text_evidence: The superego is not a copy of external authorities but a distorted, magnified, sadistic caricature of them.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The superego's cruelty exceeds any actual parental cruelty because it incorporates the child's own projected aggression.
positions.text_evidence: The superego's cruelty exceeds any actual parental cruelty because it incorporates the child's own projected aggression.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The superego demands punishment for unconscious wishes, not for actions—one is guilty for what one unconsciously desires.
positions.text_evidence: The superego demands punishment for unconscious wishes, not for actions—one is guilty for what one unconsciously desires.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Unconscious guilt is the driving force behind most self-destructive behavior.
positions.text_evidence: Unconscious guilt is the driving force behind most self-destructive behavior.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The superego uses internal guilt not to reform behavior but to extract suffering as tribute.
positions.text_evidence: The superego uses internal guilt not to reform behavior but to extract suffering as tribute.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Different neurotic configurations represent different compromises with superego demands—different ways of paying the 'tax' of suffering.
positions.text_evidence: Different neurotic configurations represent different compromises with superego demands—different ways of paying the 'tax' of suffering.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The 'corrupt superego' accepts disguised payments: symptom-suffering, self-sabotage, and masochistic character patterns all serve as currency.
positions.text_evidence: The 'corrupt superego' accepts disguised payments: symptom-suffering, self-sabotage, and masochistic character patterns all serve as currency.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Some superegos can be bribed with suffering in one domain to permit pleasure in another.
positions.text_evidence: Some superegos can be bribed with suffering in one domain to permit pleasure in another.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The 'injustice collector' is a specific character type whose entire psychic economy depends on feeling victimized.
positions.text_evidence: The 'injustice collector' is a specific character type whose entire psychic economy depends on feeling victimized.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The injustice collector unconsciously provokes mistreatment, then genuinely experiences surprise and outrage at being mistreated.
positions.text_evidence: The injustice collector unconsciously provokes mistreatment, then genuinely experiences surprise and outrage at being mistreated.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The collection of injustices serves multiple purposes: it provides masochistic satisfaction, justifies counter-aggression, and proves the original oral thesis that the world refuses.
positions.text_evidence: The collection of injustices serves multiple purposes: it provides masochistic satisfaction, justifies counter-aggression, and proves the original oral thesis that the world refuses.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Injustice collectors are drawn to situations and partners guaranteed to produce grievances.
positions.text_evidence: Injustice collectors are drawn to situations and partners guaranteed to produce grievances.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The injustice collector cannot be satisfied by fair treatment—if none is available, he will manufacture mistreatment or interpret neutral events as slights.
positions.text_evidence: The injustice collector cannot be satisfied by fair treatment—if none is available, he will manufacture mistreatment or interpret neutral events as slights.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: In marriage, injustice collectors create a self-perpetuating cycle: they provoke, collect the grievance, then use the grievance as justification for further provocation.
positions.text_evidence: In marriage, injustice collectors create a self-perpetuating cycle: they provoke, collect the grievance, then use the grievance as justification for further provocation.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Writing involves unconscious exhibitionism—the writer displays himself before an internal audience derived from early parental figures.
positions.text_evidence: Writing involves unconscious exhibitionism—the writer displays himself before an internal audience derived from early parental figures.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Writer's block occurs when the superego forbids this exhibitionism, or when guilt over unconscious meanings in the work becomes overwhelming.
positions.text_evidence: Writer's block occurs when the superego forbids this exhibitionism, or when guilt over unconscious meanings in the work becomes overwhelming.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The blocked writer is not lacking ideas but is forbidden by internal authority from expressing them.
positions.text_evidence: The blocked writer is not lacking ideas but is forbidden by internal authority from expressing them.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Many writers have an unconscious equation: writing = forbidden voyeuristic and exhibitionistic gratification.
positions.text_evidence: Many writers have an unconscious equation: writing = forbidden voyeuristic and exhibitionistic gratification.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The block often intensifies after success because success increases guilt and provokes superego retaliation.
positions.text_evidence: The block often intensifies after success because success increases guilt and provokes superego retaliation.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Writer's block frequently represents identification with the 'refusing' mother: the writer refuses to 'give' his productions just as he felt mother refused to give.
positions.text_evidence: Writer's block frequently represents identification with the 'refusing' mother: the writer refuses to 'give' his productions just as he felt mother refused to give.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The blocked writer often unconsciously identifies writing with prohibited aggressive or sexual content, regardless of the work's actual subject matter.
positions.text_evidence: The blocked writer often unconsciously identifies writing with prohibited aggressive or sexual content, regardless of the work's actual subject matter.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Block can represent a work stoppage in the inner factory—a strike against the internal slave-driving superego.
positions.text_evidence: Block can represent a work stoppage in the inner factory—a strike against the internal slave-driving superego.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Some blocked writers can only produce under conditions of suffering or deprivation, because suffering bribes the superego into permitting creative expression.
positions.text_evidence: Some blocked writers can only produce under conditions of suffering or deprivation, because suffering bribes the superego into permitting creative expression.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Writer's block is curable through analysis because it rests on unconscious conflicts that can be made conscious.
positions.text_evidence: Writer's block is curable through analysis because it rests on unconscious conflicts that can be made conscious.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Partner selection is never accidental; the unconscious chooses partners who will enable the repetition of early neurotic patterns.
positions.text_evidence: Partner selection is never accidental; the unconscious chooses partners who will enable the repetition of early neurotic patterns.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: People unconsciously select partners who will disappoint them in precisely the ways they were originally disappointed.
positions.text_evidence: People unconsciously select partners who will disappoint them in precisely the ways they were originally disappointed.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Neurotic partner choice is based not on what the partner actually is but on what the neurotic unconsciously needs the partner to be.
positions.text_evidence: Neurotic partner choice is based not on what the partner actually is but on what the neurotic unconsciously needs the partner to be.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The unhappy marriage typically involves two neurotics whose pathologies interlock—each provides the other with the frustration they unconsciously require.
positions.text_evidence: The unhappy marriage typically involves two neurotics whose pathologies interlock—each provides the other with the frustration they unconsciously require.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Many marriages are unconsciously designed to fail from the beginning; the wedding is already the first step toward divorce.
positions.text_evidence: Many marriages are unconsciously designed to fail from the beginning; the wedding is already the first step toward divorce.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The repetitive divorcer has an unconscious marital pattern that will reproduce itself regardless of the apparent differences between partners.
positions.text_evidence: The repetitive divorcer has an unconscious marital pattern that will reproduce itself regardless of the apparent differences between partners.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Marital happiness requires that both partners have resolved, to reasonable degree, their oral-masochistic conflicts.
positions.text_evidence: Marital happiness requires that both partners have resolved, to reasonable degree, their oral-masochistic conflicts.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Genuine love is based on object-cathexis (investment in the real other), not on narcissistic need (using the other for internal purposes).
positions.text_evidence: Genuine love is based on object-cathexis (investment in the real other), not on narcissistic need (using the other for internal purposes).

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Sexual adjustment in marriage depends less on technique than on the unconscious meanings sex carries for each partner.
positions.text_evidence: Sexual adjustment in marriage depends less on technique than on the unconscious meanings sex carries for each partner.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Many sexual difficulties in marriage are created by one or both partners for masochistic purposes.
positions.text_evidence: Many sexual difficulties in marriage are created by one or both partners for masochistic purposes.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: [HISTORICAL - NOW REJECTED] Bergler viewed homosexuality as a neurotic symptom rooted in oral regression and masochism.
positions.text_evidence: Bergler viewed homosexuality as a neurotic symptom rooted in oral regression and masochism. NOTE: This position has been repudiated by contemporary psychology, which recognizes homosexuality as a normal variant of human sexuality.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: [HISTORICAL - NOW REJECTED] He believed homosexuality represented a flight from the opposite sex based on unconscious fears derived from the early maternal relationship.
positions.text_evidence: He believed homosexuality represented a flight from the opposite sex based on unconscious fears derived from the early maternal relationship. NOTE: This position has been repudiated by contemporary psychology.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: [HISTORICAL - NOW REJECTED] He claimed homosexuality was treatable through psychoanalysis and was not innate.
positions.text_evidence: He claimed homosexuality was treatable through psychoanalysis and was not innate. NOTE: This position has been repudiated by contemporary psychology.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: These positions on homosexuality have been repudiated by contemporary psychology, which recognizes homosexuality as a normal variant of human sexuality.
positions.text_evidence: These positions have been repudiated by contemporary psychology, which recognizes homosexuality as a normal variant of human sexuality.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Fashion exploits universal psychological mechanisms, particularly narcissism, exhibitionism, and masochism.
positions.text_evidence: Fashion exploits universal psychological mechanisms, particularly narcissism, exhibitionism, and masochism.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Fashion's constant change reflects an unconscious need for novelty that masks oral dissatisfaction—nothing can satisfy permanently because the original oral wound cannot be healed by external means.
positions.text_evidence: Fashion's constant change reflects an unconscious need for novelty that masks oral dissatisfaction—nothing can satisfy permanently because the original oral wound cannot be healed by external means.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Fashion designers often express unconscious aggression toward women through impractical, uncomfortable, or unflattering designs.
positions.text_evidence: Fashion designers often express unconscious aggression toward women through impractical, uncomfortable, or unflattering designs.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The fashion victim is a masochistic type who submits to the sadism of arbitrary fashion dictates.
positions.text_evidence: The fashion victim is a masochistic type who submits to the sadism of arbitrary fashion dictates.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Fashion involves identification with the aggressor: by embracing the latest fashion, one joins the powerful group rather than being excluded by it.
positions.text_evidence: Fashion involves identification with the aggressor: by embracing the latest fashion, one joins the powerful group rather than being excluded by it.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The willingness to discard last season's clothes enacts the oral drama of things once desired becoming worthless once obtained.
positions.text_evidence: The willingness to discard last season's clothes enacts the oral drama of things once desired becoming worthless once obtained.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Many professional failures result not from external obstacles but from unconscious self-sabotage.
positions.text_evidence: Many professional failures result not from external obstacles but from unconscious self-sabotage.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Success can trigger intense unconscious guilt, because success feels like triumphing over parental figures.
positions.text_evidence: Success can trigger intense unconscious guilt, because success feels like triumphing over parental figures.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The 'success neurosis' describes those who cannot tolerate success and unconsciously arrange to fail just as they are about to succeed.
positions.text_evidence: The 'success neurosis' describes those who cannot tolerate success and unconsciously arrange to fail just as they are about to succeed.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Some neurotics can only function professionally under conditions of exploitation because they need to feel victimized.
positions.text_evidence: Some neurotics can only function professionally under conditions of exploitation because they need to feel victimized.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Chronic complainers about work conditions often unconsciously select or create the conditions they complain about.
positions.text_evidence: Chronic complainers about work conditions often unconsciously select or create the conditions they complain about.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The relationship with one's boss often recapitulates the relationship with early authority figures; neurotic work problems frequently reflect unresolved parental conflicts.
positions.text_evidence: The relationship with one's boss often recapitulates the relationship with early authority figures; neurotic work problems frequently reflect unresolved parental conflicts.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Talent is no protection against neurotic self-defeat; indeed, talented people often have more elaborate means of sabotaging themselves.
positions.text_evidence: Talent is no protection against neurotic self-defeat; indeed, talented people often have more elaborate means of sabotaging themselves.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Some people can only work when they are 'paid' in suffering—deprivation, poor conditions, or exploitation serve as the price that permits productivity.
positions.text_evidence: Some people can only work when they are 'paid' in suffering—deprivation, poor conditions, or exploitation serve as the price that permits productivity.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The gambler is not primarily seeking money but is unconsciously staging a drama of loss, proving the thesis that the world refuses.
positions.text_evidence: The gambler is not primarily seeking money but is unconsciously staging a drama of loss, proving the thesis that the world refuses.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Gambling provides a pure form of masochistic pleasure: the certainty of eventual loss is the unconscious aim, not the conscious hope of winning.
positions.text_evidence: Gambling provides a pure form of masochistic pleasure: the certainty of eventual loss is the unconscious aim, not the conscious hope of winning.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The gambler is in rebellion against logic and reality—against the 'reality principle' that represents parental authority.
positions.text_evidence: The gambler is in rebellion against logic and reality—against the 'reality principle' that represents parental authority.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The excitement of gambling represents a manic defense against underlying depression.
positions.text_evidence: The excitement of gambling represents a manic defense against underlying depression.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Addiction represents oral regression to a stage where a substance replaces the mother; the addict seeks the illusion of oral fusion.
positions.text_evidence: Addiction represents oral regression to a stage where a substance replaces the mother; the addict seeks the illusion of oral fusion.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Addictive patterns inevitably become self-punishing, which serves the masochistic component of the underlying neurosis.
positions.text_evidence: Addictive patterns inevitably become self-punishing, which serves the masochistic component of the underlying neurosis.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The addict's relationship to the substance replicates the ambivalent oral relationship: desperate need combined with rage at dependency.
positions.text_evidence: The addict's relationship to the substance replicates the ambivalent oral relationship: desperate need combined with rage at dependency.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Pseudo-aggression and Don Juanism often mask underlying passivity and unconscious feminine identification.
positions.text_evidence: Pseudo-aggression and Don Juanism often mask underlying passivity and unconscious feminine identification.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The compulsive seducer is not driven by sexual desire but by the need to prove masculinity he unconsciously doubts.
positions.text_evidence: The compulsive seducer is not driven by sexual desire but by the need to prove masculinity he unconsciously doubts.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The man who needs to dominate women is defending against terror of women derived from the early maternal relationship.
positions.text_evidence: The man who needs to dominate women is defending against terror of women derived from the early maternal relationship.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Impotence frequently results from unconscious prohibition—the superego forbids sexual success, often due to incestuous meanings.
positions.text_evidence: Impotence frequently results from unconscious prohibition—the superego forbids sexual success, often due to incestuous meanings.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The sadistic man toward women is expressing displaced aggression originally directed at the mother.
positions.text_evidence: The sadistic man toward women is expressing displaced aggression originally directed at the mother.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Men who repeatedly choose 'impossible' women are unconsciously arranging for romantic failure.
positions.text_evidence: Men who repeatedly choose 'impossible' women are unconsciously arranging for romantic failure.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Exaggerated masculinity often indicates fixation at the phallic level covering deeper oral-passive wishes.
positions.text_evidence: Exaggerated masculinity often indicates fixation at the phallic level covering deeper oral-passive wishes.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Frigidity is not a lack of capacity for pleasure but an active inhibition based on unconscious conflict.
positions.text_evidence: Frigidity is not a lack of capacity for pleasure but an active inhibition based on unconscious conflict.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The frigid woman is typically punishing men (displacing aggression from father), punishing herself (superego tribute), or both.
positions.text_evidence: The frigid woman is typically punishing men (displacing aggression from father), punishing herself (superego tribute), or both.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Women who choose unsuitable men are not making errors of judgment but unconscious choices guaranteeing disappointment.
positions.text_evidence: Women who choose unsuitable men are not making errors of judgment but unconscious choices guaranteeing disappointment.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Masochistic submission in women often represents aggression turned inward, combined with repetition of early submission to maternal authority.
positions.text_evidence: Masochistic submission in women often represents aggression turned inward, combined with repetition of early submission to maternal authority.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Competition with men sometimes represents identification with the aggressor combined with revenge for early narcissistic injuries.
positions.text_evidence: Competition with men sometimes represents identification with the aggressor combined with revenge for early narcissistic injuries.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The woman who sabotages her husband's success may be expressing displaced aggression, or may need to maintain his dependency to assuage her own anxieties.
positions.text_evidence: The woman who sabotages her husband's success may be expressing displaced aggression, or may need to maintain his dependency to assuage her own anxieties.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Neurosis and psychosis differ in degree of ego damage and relationship to reality—neurosis involves symptom formation while maintaining reality testing; psychosis involves ego fragmentation.
positions.text_evidence: Neurosis and psychosis differ in degree of ego damage and relationship to reality—neurosis involves symptom formation while maintaining reality testing; psychosis involves ego fragmentation.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The specific form of neurosis depends on the developmental point of fixation and the specific defense mechanisms employed.
positions.text_evidence: The specific form of neurosis depends on the developmental point of fixation and the specific defense mechanisms employed.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Hysteria primarily employs repression and involves conflicts centered on genital-Oedipal themes, but with underlying oral components.
positions.text_evidence: Hysteria primarily employs repression and involves conflicts centered on genital-Oedipal themes, but with underlying oral components.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Obsessional neurosis employs isolation, undoing, and reaction formation; it appears anal but often masks oral conflicts.
positions.text_evidence: Obsessional neurosis employs isolation, undoing, and reaction formation; it appears anal but often masks oral conflicts.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Depression represents aggression turned against the self following real or imagined loss of a love object with whom one is ambivalently identified.
positions.text_evidence: Depression represents aggression turned against the self following real or imagined loss of a love object with whom one is ambivalently identified.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Perversions represent fixations at component sexual drives that failed to integrate under genital primacy.
positions.text_evidence: Perversions represent fixations at component sexual drives that failed to integrate under genital primacy.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Character neurosis is more entrenched than symptom neurosis because the pathology is experienced as part of the self rather than as a foreign symptom.
positions.text_evidence: Character neurosis is more entrenched than symptom neurosis because the pathology is experienced as part of the self rather than as a foreign symptom.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Neurosis is not destiny; unconscious conflicts made conscious lose their determining power.
positions.text_evidence: Neurosis is not destiny; unconscious conflicts made conscious lose their determining power.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Analysis works by making the unconscious conscious, thereby expanding the ego's domain at the expense of the id and superego.
positions.text_evidence: Analysis works by making the unconscious conscious, thereby expanding the ego's domain at the expense of the id and superego.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The patient's resistance to cure is itself a manifestation of the neurosis—part of him clings to his suffering.
positions.text_evidence: The patient's resistance to cure is itself a manifestation of the neurosis—part of him clings to his suffering.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Genuine cure requires not just intellectual insight but emotional working-through of unconscious conflicts.
positions.text_evidence: Genuine cure requires not just intellectual insight but emotional working-through of unconscious conflicts.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Many patients seek not cure but more effective neurosis—a version of their pathology that works better.
positions.text_evidence: Many patients seek not cure but more effective neurosis—a version of their pathology that works better.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Short-term interventions cannot address character neurosis, which requires extended analytic work.
positions.text_evidence: Short-term interventions cannot address character neurosis, which requires extended analytic work.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The goal of treatment is not happiness but freedom from neurotic compulsion—what one does with that freedom is one's own affair.
positions.text_evidence: The goal of treatment is not happiness but freedom from neurotic compulsion—what one does with that freedom is one's own affair.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The pre-Oedipal oral phase (0-18 months) is more fundamental to neurosis than the Oedipal phase (3-5...
positions.text_evidence: The pre-Oedipal oral phase (0-18 months) is more fundamental to neurosis than the Oedipal phase (3-5 years).

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: There is only one "basic neurosis"—repressed masochistic attachment to the fantasied earliest "bad m...
positions.text_evidence: There is only one "basic neurosis"—repressed masochistic attachment to the fantasied earliest "bad mother."

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Psychic masochism is the unconscious pursuit of "pleasure in displeasure."
positions.text_evidence: Psychic masochism is the unconscious pursuit of "pleasure in displeasure."

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Psychic masochism constitutes "the life-blood of neurosis" and is in fact the basic neurosis.
positions.text_evidence: Psychic masochism constitutes "the life-blood of neurosis" and is in fact the basic neurosis.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Neurotic equals psychic masochist—every neurotic is fundamentally a psychic masochist.
positions.text_evidence: Neurotic equals psychic masochist—every neurotic is fundamentally a psychic masochist.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The superego is the "real master of the personality."
positions.text_evidence: The superego is the "real master of the personality."

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The superego operates as "torture for torture's sake."
positions.text_evidence: The superego operates as "torture for torture's sake."

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: The punitive part of the superego (Daimonion) uses the ego ideal to torture the ego.
positions.text_evidence: The punitive part of the superego (Daimonion) uses the ego ideal to torture the ego.

positions.author: Edmund Bergler
positions.title: Punishment itself becomes masochized—the neurotic derives unconscious pleasure from being punished.
positions.text_evidence: Punishment itself becomes masochized—the neurotic derives unconscious pleasure from being punished.
